summary: Spolsky criticizes 'architecture astronauts' who fixate on technical architecture patterns while completely missing the user value that makes products successful. He uses Napster as an example: its value wasn't peer-to-peer technology, but the ability to instantly find and listen to any song. He observes how this architectural fixation spawns an entire ecosystem of hype — conferences, VC funds — followed by predictable backlash, all disconnected from what actually matters to users.
key_points: Architecture astronauts strip away user value and fixate on technical patterns, missing what makes products actually compelling
Napster's success was about instant music access, not peer-to-peer architecture — but the tech industry obsessed over the latter
Architectural hype cycles (conferences, VC funds, backlash articles) are self-reinforcing and disconnected from real product value
money_quotes: Your typical architecture astronaut will take a fact like 'Napster is a peer-to-peer service for downloading music' and ignore everything but the architecture, thinking it's interesting because it's peer to peer, completely missing the point that it's interesting because you can type the name of a song and listen to it right away.

Suddenly you have peer-to-peer conferences, peer-to-peer venture capital funds, and even peer-to-peer backlash with the imbecile business journalists dripping with glee as they copy each other's stories.
key_insight: Products succeed because of the problems they solve for users, not because of the architectural patterns they employ — but the tech industry chronically confuses the two.
